question.text: Scenario: Sanjay Gupta is valuing Ratan Industries using the Gordon Growth (DDM) model. The company paid a dividend of Rs.10 per share last year. The dividend is expected to grow at 5% perpetually. The cost of equity (required rate of return) is 12%. The current market price is Rs.140. He also has a comparable peer, Lakshmi Corp, which trades at PE of 14x and EV/EBITDA of 9x. Ratan Industries' EPS is Rs.15 and EBITDA per share is Rs.25. Ratan's EV per share is Rs.200. Question 5 of 5: All three valuation methods (DDM, PE, EV/EBITDA) suggest Ratan Industries is:

answer.text: Significantly overvalued and should be sold immediately
Moderately undervalued across all methods — DDM (Rs.150 vs Rs.140), PE (Rs.210 vs Rs.140), EV/EBITDA (Rs.225 vs Rs.200) — supporting a Buy recommendation
Fairly valued with no action needed
Cannot be determined as the methods give contradictory results

explanation.text: **The Logic:** All three methods point to undervaluation: DDM gives Rs.150, PE gives Rs.210, EV/EBITDA gives Rs.225. Convergence across multiple methods strengthens the Buy case. **The Trap:** The methods are consistent in direction (all show undervaluation), even though absolute values differ (normal for different approaches). **Cross-Exam Context:** Tested in NISM XV Ch 10. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** When multiple valuation methods agree on direction, the investment thesis is more robust. The range (Rs.150-225) gives a valuation band.
